body:
Financial Survival During Kundalini Crisis: Practical Money Management

You're probably reading this because you're scared. You're feeling things you can't explain, seeing things that aren't there, or maybe you just can't function like you used to. And on top of all that, you're worried about money. Yeah, I get it. I've been there. That deep, cold dread when you realize your life, your job, your ability to even get out of bed, it's all up in the air. This isn't some mystical journey for you right now, it's a fight for survival. And part of that fight is making sure you don't end up homeless or starving.

Look, when you're in the thick of a kundalini crisis, or any kind of spiritual emergency, your brain isn't working right. You're not thinking straight. You're probably having visions, feeling intense energy, or just completely dissociated from reality. Making rational financial decisions? Forget about it. But you have to try. You have to put some basic things in place, even if it feels impossible.

Acceptance: This Is Your New Reality, For Now

The first thing you gotta do, as hard as it is, is accept that your life has changed. Maybe temporarily, maybe for a long time. You can't pretend you're going to snap back to normal next week and pick up where you left off. That thinking will screw you over financially. You need to acknowledge that your income, your spending habits, your ability to work - it's all different now. This isn't about giving up; it's about being realistic so you can actually survive.

I remember thinking, "This will pass, I just need a few days." A few days turned into weeks, then months, then years. And during that time, I made some really dumb financial choices because I was in denial. Don't be me. Be smarter.

Assess Your Immediate Financial Situation - The Cold, Hard Facts

You need to know exactly where you stand. Even if it makes you sick to your stomach, you need to look at the numbers. Get a pen and paper, or open a spreadsheet. Don't avoid this. It's like checking your wounds - it hurts, but you need to know how bad it is so you can treat it.

  • How much cash do you have right now? In your bank account, under your mattress, everywhere.
  • What are your absolute, non-negotiable monthly expenses? Rent/mortgage, utilities (gas, electric, water), basic food, essential medication. Forget the Netflix, forget the fancy coffee. Just the bare bones.
  • What's coming in? Your last paycheck, any unemployment benefits, disability? Be honest.
  • What debts do you have? Credit cards, loans, medical bills? Just list them out.

This is your baseline. This is what you're working with. It might be terrifying, but knowing is better than guessing. Guessing leads to panic. Knowing leads to a plan, however small.

Cut Everything You Can - Ruthlessly

This is where you become a financial samurai. Every single non-essential expense needs to go. Right now. You can always add things back later when you're stable. But for now, it's survival mode.

  • Subscriptions: Netflix, Spotify, gym memberships, apps - cancel them all. You don't need them.
  • Eating out/Takeout: This is a huge money drain. You need to cook at home. Simple, cheap meals. Rice, beans, pasta, frozen veggies. It's not gourmet, but it keeps you alive.
  • Unnecessary Shopping: Clothes, gadgets, impulse buys. Stop it. You can't afford it.
  • Transportation: Can you walk or bike instead of driving? Can you use public transport? Every little bit helps.
  • Hobbies: If your hobbies cost money, put them on hold.

It's going to suck. You're going to feel deprived. But remember why you're doing this: to keep a roof over your head and food in your belly while your entire being is rearranging itself. It's a temporary sacrifice.

Communicate and Ask for Help - Swallow Your Pride

This is one of the hardest parts, especially if you're someone who's always been independent. But you can't do this alone. You're in a crisis. People need to know.

  • Family/Friends: If you have people you trust, tell them what's happening. Not necessarily all the spiritual stuff - they might not get it - but tell them you're unwell and need help. Can they lend you money? Can they bring you food? Can you stay with them for a bit to save on rent? This isn't weakness; it's smart.
  • Creditors: If you're going to miss payments, call them. Don't ignore them. Many companies have hardship programs. They might defer payments, reduce interest, or work out a payment plan. It's better to call and explain than to just let it go to collections.
  • Landlord/Mortgage Company: Same thing. If you can't pay, talk to them. They might be willing to work with you, especially if you've been a good tenant/payer before. Eviction is a nightmare you don't need right now.

This is where you might feel exposed, vulnerable. But you're already exposed. You're already vulnerable. So drop the ego and ask for what you need. People often want to help, they just don't know how.

For some deeper context on what you might be going through, you might find some comfort in Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link). It helps to know you're not the only one who's had their world turned upside down.

Explore Social Safety Nets - They're There for a Reason

Depending on where you live, there are resources available. They might not be perfect, and applying for them can be a pain, especially when you're not well, but they can be life-savers.

  • Unemployment Benefits: If you lost your job or had to quit because you couldn't function, look into this.
  • Disability Benefits: If this is a prolonged crisis and you truly can't work, this is an option. It's usually a long process, but it's worth starting.
  • Food Stamps/SNAP: Helps with groceries. Don't be ashamed to use them.
  • Medical Assistance: If you don't have insurance or can't afford your medical bills, look into state or federal programs. You might need to see doctors, even if you feel like they don't understand what's happening.
  • Local Charities/Food Banks: Many communities have organizations that can help with food, utility bills, or even temporary housing. Do a quick online search for "food banks near me" or "utility assistance [your city/state]".

Yeah, it's paperwork, it's phone calls, it's dealing with bureaucracy. And when you're barely holding it together, it feels like climbing Mount Everest. But every little bit of support you can get frees up what little money you have for other essentials.

Consider Temporary Work - If You Can Manage It

This is a big "if." If you're deep in crisis, working might be impossible. But if you have periods of clarity, or if your symptoms aren't completely debilitating, think about what you *can* do.

  • Gig Work: Things like online surveys, data entry, proofreading (if your mind is clear enough), or even delivering groceries. These often have flexible hours, so you can work when you're able and rest when you're not.
  • Selling Stuff: Do you have anything you don't need? Clothes, old electronics, furniture? Sell it on eBay, Facebook Marketplace, Craigslist. Every dollar helps.
  • Part-time, low-stress jobs: Maybe a few hours a week at a local store, or something that doesn't require a lot of mental heavy lifting. Don't push yourself too hard, but if you can manage a few hours, it makes a difference.

Don't try to go back to your old demanding job if you're not ready. That'll just make things worse, financially and mentally. Be realistic about your current capacity.

Understanding the actual physical and mental changes happening can sometimes make the financial struggle a bit more bearable, because you know it's not "just you." The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) offers some clinical perspectives that might help you feel less alone in the wild ride your body is on.

Simplify Your Life - Drastically

This isn't just about money; it's about reducing stress and external demands. When your inner world is chaos, you need your outer world to be as simple and calm as possible.

  • Declutter: Get rid of things. Less stuff means less to worry about, less to clean, less to maintain. And you might even sell some of it.
  • Minimize commitments: Say no to things that drain your energy or time. You need all your resources for healing.
  • Automate bills: If you can, set up automatic payments for your essential bills so you don't forget them during periods of fogginess. Just make sure you have the money in the account.
  • Create a simple routine: Even if it's just waking up, eating, resting, and going to bed. Structure helps when everything else feels unstructured.

Protect Your Assets - If You Have Any

If you're lucky enough to have savings, investments, or property, you need to protect them. Don't make impulsive decisions when you're in a vulnerable state. I've seen people blow through their life savings during a spiritual crisis, convinced they didn't need money anymore, or that some guru would provide, or that they were "beyond" material things. Don't fall for that trap. You still live in this world, and this world requires money.

  • Don't touch retirement funds unless it's truly life or death. The penalties are steep.
  • Be wary of "spiritual" investments or schemes. When you're open and vulnerable, you're a target for scammers. If it sounds too good to be true, it is.
  • Consider giving a trusted family member limited access or oversight. If you truly can't trust yourself with financial decisions, and you have someone you absolutely trust, talk to them about helping manage your money for a period. This is a big step, but sometimes necessary.

It's a tough road, and sometimes you feel like you're losing your mind. But knowing that others have been through similar experiences can be a lifeline. Books like The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) or The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) can provide some much-needed perspective and validation that what you're experiencing is real, even if it feels insane.

Long-Term Vision (When You Can Even Think About It)

Once you've stabilized a bit, and you have some breathing room, start thinking about the future. It doesn't have to be a grand plan, just some small steps.

  • Re-evaluate your skills: What can you do now? What new skills do you need to learn? Many people find their old careers don't fit anymore after a crisis.
  • Build a new support network: Not just for money, but for emotional support. People who get it, or at least try to.
  • Save a small emergency fund: Even $100 saved is better than nothing. Start small.

This isn't just about money, really. It's about stability. When your inner world is in upheaval, having some external stability - knowing you won't starve, knowing you have a place to sleep - it makes a huge difference. It frees up some mental space to actually *deal* with what's happening inside you.

So yeah, it's hard. It's going to be messy. You're going to make mistakes. But you're strong enough to get through this. Take it one day at a time, one decision at a time. And don't forget to breathe. You're still here. That's a start.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
